data.title: LexinFintech: Still Bullish Even With Evolving Landscape
data.description:
LexinFintech Holdings (NASDAQ:LX) has experienced an incredible stock run since late-2024. However, the evolving economic landscape, more specifically around rising tensions between the U.S. and China, has caused over 30% pullback in the stock.

In a more normalized geopolitical environment, I believe this company has a strong foundation to support growth. Nevertheless, we are no longer in a normalized environment, and exogenous factors are starting to form into headwinds.

LexinFintech is a Chinese fintech that provides consumer financial services, primarily through its platform “Fenqile” which roughly translates to “happy installment” in Chinese. The company has been a clear beneficiary of the rise of buy-now-pay-later services, which has only accelerated since the COVID-fueled spending time period. In a more challenging spending environment, consumers may actually increase their usage of BNPL, as installment payments make larger purchases easier to pay.

Yes, there are still significant risks with the company given the combination of tense U.S.-China relations and lower consumer confidence. However, I believe this pullback presents a good buying opportunity for long-term focused investors.

Examining the Fundamentals

In my last article on LexinFintech, I discussed how this could be a multi-year bullish story, noting that I would view any pullbacks as an attractive entry point.

I remain bullish on the long-term trajectory for the company, however, given some of the broader macro challenges and the stock price potentially exceeding growth expectations, I am more hesitant to be ultra-bullish at current levels. Remaining bullish long-term and would use any pullbacks as an attractive entry point.

Buy-now-pay-later solutions have continued to grow in popularity in recent years, with younger generations often preferring this over traditional credit cards. U.S.-based competitors such as Affirm, Klarna, and Afterpay have gained significant traction, and LexinFintech is aiming to do the same with the Chinese consumer base.

According to a report by ResearchAndMarkets, the BNPL market in China grew at a nearly 20% CAGR from 2021-2024, demonstrated the strong tailwinds seen from COVID spending. Growth is expected to be at a more much subdued ~9% CAGR from 2025-2030.

Similar to other BNPL ecosystems, LexinFintech has established strong relationships with both the merchants and the consumers. The unique part of LexinFintech is their focus on the underbanked community, which often does not have access to traditional credit methods, such as credit cards, and thus must rely on installment loans like BNPL in order to finance larger transactions.

The company’s consumer focus is also much more tech-savvy, and the focus on this next-generation consumer may give LexinFintech the long-term competitive advantage. The company has built out an ecosystem of >3k merchant partners and >228 million users.

LexinFintech reported their Q4-2024 results a few weeks ago, which included revenue growth of 4% yoy. However, the company’s focus in recent quarters has been more on improving credit quality, thus accelerating profitability. For the quarter, adjusted net income grew an impressive 38% yoy.

The company is focusing more on driving recurring customer base, and this is evident with 85.3% of loans during Q4 coming from repeated borrowers' contribution. As with many financial technology companies, it is much easier and cheaper to expand with existing customers than it is to acquire new ones.

And acquiring new customers has been a bit of a challenge for LexinFintech. Yes, the 228 million user base is a significant number, and this did grow 9% yoy. However, the number of active users during Q4 was only 4.7 million, declining 1% yoy. It is not uncommon for BNPL ecosystems to have significantly more total users compared to its active user base. However, the fact that LexinFintech’s active user base is declining is a bit of a concern.

From a credit quality standpoint, trends have been consistent, with first payment default rate (30+ days) for new loan originations staying below 1%. Delinquency ratio for 90+ days was 3.6%, slightly lower than the 3.7% observed during Q3-2024.

As mentioned above, the macro environment is often the biggest swing factor for financial technology companies. A general slowdown in consumer spending typically correlates with lower loan originations, thus less growth.

LexinFintech appears to be taking a more conservative approach with their guidance and assumes total loan origination growth of flat to single-digit yoy during 2025. Some of management’s additional commentary is below.

Looking ahead to 2025, amid the current macro and industry environment, we will continue to adhere to prudent operating strategy, prioritizing risk management, and driving further de-risking and asset structure optimization. We are confident in achieving significant profit growth this year. We will strengthen our differentiated offerings in credit lines and pricings and refine our operating systems to meet the diverse financial needs of our customers at all levels, delivering high-quality services throughout their lifecycle.

The heightened focus on credit quality could prove to be extremely important in 2025.

Since the company reported earnings on March 18, a lot has changed with the macro. Most importantly, the U.S. and China have initiated tariffs, further escalating the already heightened geopolitical relationship. Although the ultimate outcome of tariffs and the impact on consumer spending is uncertain, several US-based banks with global reach have warned that consumer spending may slow down as a result of tariff uncertainty.

Even just one month removed from earnings, it appears the risk to growth is higher now. Management has taken the right steps to focus on credit quality and asset optimization; however, lower loan origination growth will negatively impact the business in 2025.

Valuation

As outlined above, there are some risks to the company’s 2025 guidance that was provided just one month ago. However, the stock has already pulled back over 30% from recent highs as investors have re-set their expectations around LexinFintech.

The challenging part from here is determining how much downside is left to go.

At face value, the company’s valuation at ~4x EPS appears very cheap. Even if loan originations turn negative for the year, the company’s internal cost improvements could still potentially yield adjusted net income growth. Investors are not likely banking on this scenario to happen in 2025, and given that the company only just reported, we are not likely to get another financial update from LexinFintech for another ~2 months.

For now, the recent pullback presents an opportunity to cautiously build up a position in the name. The 30% pullback has already discounted 2025 financial expectations, and with the market settling down a bit over the past week, we may have seen the worst already.

Risks

The biggest risk to be aware of is the general macro environment and rising tensions between the U.S. and China. Given LexinFintech’s focus on the Chinese consumer, a deteriorating relationship between the two countries could cause accelerated consumer spending pressure. Companies with China exposure often face sentiment headwinds when the geopolitical relationship becomes more strained.

Additionally, and without bringing politics into the conversation, there have already been a lot of changes within the current U.S. Administration. If this Administration were to restrict investments into China-based companies, this would be a material detriment to LexinFintech.

Another risk is the ongoing competition. This includes traditional financial institutions and digital-native commerce platforms. Larger banks such as Ant Financial, JD Finance, WeChat, Baidu, and Tencent all compete in the BNPL market.

Finally, small-cap stocks can often have a trading and liquidity issue. When looking at data from Yahoo Finance, the stock has traded in a very wide 52-week range of $1.57-$11.64, signaling the high volatility. On average, ~4.3 million shares trade each day, representing ~$30 million of trading volume, or ~2.6% of the company's market cap. The rule of thumb is that anything above 2% is generally considered to have good liquidity.

data.title: The Prime Contractor Of The New Energy Era: The Quanta Case
data.description:
Introduction

Quanta Services (NYSE:PWR) is the largest player in the energy and communications infrastructure market throughout North America. With more than 200 combined contractors and a team of over 58,000 people, the company is able to take on everything from the most complex design to urgent disaster recovery.

In 2024, revenue increased by 13.4% to $23.67 billion, and the total backlog—those orders that are already in progress or about to be signed—exceeded $34.5 billion. This is, in fact, the visibility of revenue for several years ahead. To understand the scale: Quanta accounts for about 15% of all external contracting costs in the US energy sector. This is three times more than the nearest competitor. Now the company is right in the epicenter of a huge investment cycle for infrastructure renovation.

Business model

Quanta Services is not just a contractor, but an entire ecosystem that builds the infrastructure of the future. The company has a full-fledged technological approach to energy, communications, and integrated infrastructure construction. Electric grids, wind farms, fiber optics, gas distribution, oil refining — all this is really being closed by Quanta Services. In 2024, 83% of Quanta's revenue came from fixed and unit-price contracts, which, on the one hand, provide predictability and on the other hand, maintain good margins. The company also has many long-term framework agreements with major customers, so the company's order flow is stable.

Previously, the company was mainly engaged in energy, but now it has data centers, telecom, and industrial facilities in its arsenal. In 2024, they even changed the reporting structure: they combined electrics and renewable energy into one segment—Electric Infrastructure Solutions. The second segment, Underground Utility & Infrastructure—is about gas, oil, and the entire industrial infrastructure. This business architecture allows you to close the entire cycle—from design to final completion of the facility—without involving third-party contractors.

From the point of view of capital, their model is really effective. In 2024, CapEx accounted for about 2.5% of revenue, which is a very low level for such a large—scale business. Quanta does not burn money on a fleet of vehicles, it works on a point-by-point basis: some of the equipment is rented and some is purchased as needed. The main expenses are people, materials, and subcontracting. This structure makes the business as adaptive as possible. If the market slows down, they quickly reduce costs. If, on the contrary, there is a surge in demand, they scale up calmly.

Competitors

There are many players in the infrastructure services market in North America, and there are plenty of directions, but Quanta Services has almost no direct competitors at its level. The closest in scale and profile is MasTec (MTZ). They are also building power grids, telecom, and pipelines, but at the same time, they focus on traditional energy. Quanta, especially after the Blattner takeover, has gone deep into renewable energy and electricity in general, while MasTec continues to focus on oil, gas, and main pipes. Yes, they overlap in terms of clients — Duke Energy (DUK) and Exelon (EXC) — but they often work in different parts of the same project.

There is also MYR Group (MYRG), which does well in city tenders and can compete on prices, especially in small projects. However, when it comes to large—scale stories like interstate transmission lines or integrated power systems, Quanta is out of competition in terms of scale and resources. There is also competition in the telecom segment, such as Dycom (DY), especially in fiber and 5G. But the niche itself is so overheated by demand, especially in rural areas, that competition there is more of a formality: there are enough orders for everyone. Quanta is simply expanding its product line here and working under the same contracts as in the energy sector.

As for oil and gas, Quanta used to enter main pipelines, but in recent years it has made a U-turn towards distribution networks. There is less competition, the volumes are stable, and the load is predictable. They feel confident in this niche, while players like Primoris (PRIM) or Fluor (FLR) focus more on "big" projects like refineries or LNG terminals.

In renewable energy, after the purchase of Blattner, Quanta simply became an absolute leader. The main competitors are Mortenson, Acciona (OTCPK:ACXIF), and Orsted, but they often do not have the same comprehensive competence.: They build wind farms, but they don't build power lines or substations. And Quanta can close the entire facility from the foundation to the connection to the network. And it's really valuable.

Politics in 2025 has added difficulties: the Trump administration has turned rather sharply towards traditional energy. The issuance of "green" subsidies and licenses for offshore wind turbines has been slowed down. If the renewable energy market freezes, it will affect the company's growth, especially in terms of new projects. At the same time, regulators such as FERC have simplified the approval process for fossil fuel pipelines and infrastructure under new leadership. These developments create more favorable conditions for companies involved in oil and gas construction. Major natural gas transportation projects, the construction of export LNG terminals, and the modernization of refineries can be expected to revive. If such an infrastructure boom in the traditional energy sector begins, MasTec and similar players (Primoris, engineering firms) will be in a winning position, as they have a profile close to the needs of oil and gas companies, Quanta will be more difficult.

But, on the other hand, the states continue to push their decarbonization programs, the renewable energy economy is already self-sufficient, and large customers are still planning to switch to clean energy. In addition, everyone needs an electric infrastructure — both those who install wind turbines and those who build data centers. If gas production starts to increase, it will also mean more substations, more lines, and more work.

Financials

Revenue increased from $11.2 billion in 2020 to $23.67 billion in 2024, more than doubling in four years. This trend was driven by growth in the electricity and renewable energy segments, as well as the integration of new assets, despite a moderate decline in the oil and gas sector. Today, almost half of the company's revenue is generated by electric grids, a third by renewable energy, and about 20% by underground infrastructure. At the same time, the current portfolio of orders — $34.5 billion, including $17.3 billion of short—term contracts—provides excellent visibility of future revenue.

For 2025, I expect revenue of $26.47 billion, which corresponds to an increase of almost 12%. This growth is supported by the investment cycle in infrastructure — from new transmission lines and charging stations to the replacement of old gas pipelines — and Quanta's high share of industry costs. My forecast is generally consistent with the consensus forecast, which expects revenue in 2025 in the range of $26.70-27.01 billion.

The Quanta Services business, like most contractors, traditionally operates with low margins, but this does not prevent the company from showing stable profit growth. In 2024, the gross margin reached 14.8% and the operating margin reached 5.5%, which is slightly higher than previous levels due to increased scale, high—quality contract execution, and a profitable project structure. In 2025, I expect EBITDA of about $2.21 billion with a margin of 8.3%, taking into account the integration of CEI and wage pressures. At the same time, net profit, according to my forecast, will grow to $952 million in 2025 and exceed $1.15 billion by 2027.

At the end of 2024, the company's net debt was $3.42 billion, with a net debt/EBITDA ratio of 1.83x. Most of the debt is long—term fixed-rate bonds, which minimizes the risks from rising interest rates. Quanta also has an almost unused credit facility worth $2.5 billion, which provides high liquidity. Management explicitly states the priority of deleveraging, and in 2024 the company reduced its debt by $300 million after the purchase of CEI.

The Quanta Services business, although associated with large—scale infrastructure construction, remains relatively low-capital-intensive—the main investments go into special equipment, a fleet, and tools for crews. In 2024, capital investments amounted to $604 million (2.5% of total revenue). Some of the funds were spent on upgrading equipment after stressful years of disaster recovery. I expect that in 2025 it will be at a similar level with investments of about $658 million. Part of the cost is offset by the sale of old equipment: in 2024, Quanta returned about $78 million through the sale of equipment. This allows us to keep net CapEx closer to 2% of revenue, which distinguishes Quanta from capital-intensive industries.

In 2024, the company recorded a record FCF of $1.68 billion, which was made possible by an increase in operating profit and effective working capital management, in particular, a reduction in accounts receivable and improved settlement conditions for large contracts. I expect FCF to adjust slightly down to $1.31 billion in 2025 as the normal level of current assets is restored, but it will start growing again in 2026, exceeding $2 billion by 2028. The main source of cash flow is operating profit, not one—time transactions, which indicates its sustainability.

In fiscal year 2024, the company paid $40 million in dividends, or $0.27 per share, with a yield of about 0.14%. In 2025, according to the market consensus forecast, the company will allocate about $60 million for dividends or $0.41 per share with a yield of 0.15%.

Valuation

Now let's move on to the most painful part of my analysis – the assessment. The company's shares have grown by 9.70% over the past year due to good financial results, but at the same time, a price correction has begun since the beginning of the year. But even with the correction, the company's share price looks highly overvalued.

Firstly, the company trades at a significant premium to its historical averages and medians in terms of multipliers: the premium for P/E is 14%, for P/S – 19%, and for EV/EBITDA – 26%

Compared to peers, the company also trades at premiums in terms of P/E, P/B, P/S, and EV/EBITDA multipliers, as shown in the table below. At the same time, for example, in terms of profitability, the company is inferior to the average values – most peers are more profitable. The only advantage is the high rate of revenue growth over the past 5 years – here the company is second only to Comfort Systems (FIX).

In my forecast of the target price, I use three estimation methods: DCF (estimated weight – 40%), the target value of P/E, P/S, and EV/EBITDA with estimated weights of 20%. In the basic DCF valuation scenario, I assume a growth rate of 3.0% in the terminal period and a weighted average cost of capital of 8.34%. I chose TGR based on the long-term global economic growth rate of 2-3% per year. The equity risk premium is taken from the Damodaran table. The current 10-year Treasury Rate is used as the risk-free rate.

The target P/E multiplier for 2025 is 39.78x, P/S is 1.43x, and EV/EBITDA is 18.67x (I took the averages for the last five years).

According to my calculations, the final fair valuation of the company's shares is $210.93, which is 22.75% lower than the current price. Indeed, I believe that despite the high-quality business and high-growth rates, the company's shares are highly overvalued and at this price, I am not ready to recommend it to those investors who are currently eyeing Quanta. But I also cannot recommend that Quanta securities holders sell them, as this is a really high–quality company that is worth investing in—but at a reasonable price. Therefore, I assign the company a "Hold" rating.

Key risks

The main risk is dependence on large infrastructure investments, especially in the energy and renewable energy sectors. With the advent of Trump, federal support for "green" projects is beginning to be curtailed, which means that some of those orders that were hanging in the backlog may either be delayed or not reach the execution phase at all. At the same time, most of Quanta's revenue is based on fixed or unit contracts, where revaluation of estimates or postponement of deadlines is a blow to margins.

And given how fast salaries in construction are growing now and how badly there are not enough qualified people, the cost can eat up more profits than planned. In segments such as telecom or distribution networks, the competition is very fierce, and the winner is not always the one who is better, but often the one who is cheaper. If we add a possible recession to this, where utilities and energy companies will start cutting budgets, the growth rate of Quanta can easily slow down. If this happens against the background of large investment projects or fresh M&A, then FCF compression may temporarily worsen the financial situation.

Key growth drivers

The main driver is the global trend towards electrification of everything. Electricity consumption is growing, the number of data centers is increasing, electric vehicles require charging, renewable energy projects continue to connect to the grid—and all this requires not just modernization, but a large-scale restructuring of the entire energy infrastructure. Quanta is able to build everything from wind farms to substations and power transmission lines, on a turnkey basis, without involving a dozen contractors. At the same time, the company has a good financial position—FCF is steadily growing, the debt burden is under control, and this creates a foundation for both organic growth and new acquisition deals. The experience with CEI and Blattner has already shown that the company can scale without loss of quality. Long-term contracts, high repeatability of orders, and strong positions in key niches ensure strong revenue visibility for years to come.

Conclusion

Quanta Services enters 2025 in excellent shape. The company shows steady revenue growth, high order workload, and improved profitability. Cash flows are at a historic peak and more than cover the needs of the business. The balance sheet remains strong – although the debt has grown due to acquisitions, it is quite controllable, and its reduction has already begun. According to my calculations, the final fair valuation of the company's shares is $210.93, which is 22.75% lower than the current price. Indeed, I believe that despite the high-quality business and high-growth rates, the company's shares are highly overvalued and at this price, I am not ready to recommend it to those investors who are currently eyeing Quanta. But I also cannot recommend that Quanta securities holders sell them, as this is a really high–quality company that is worth investing in—but at a reasonable price. Therefore, I assign the company a "Hold" rating.

data.title: Emerging Markets Debt: 4 Questions For Investors
data.description:
The emerging markets (EM) debt investable universe is large, diverse, and often misunderstood. While it offers the potential for higher returns, it also comes with higher risks. To unlock alpha opportunities, it’s important to have a full understanding of this unique asset class.

What is EM Debt?

The EM debt investable universe is composed of fixed-income securities issued by EM governments and companies. Overall, EM debt consists of 900 issuers from more than 90 countries.

Securities can be issued in hard currencies or local currencies, and there are four universes within EM debt: hard currency sovereign debt, hard currency frontier markets debt, hard currency corporate debt, and local currency sovereign debt.

Hard currency debt is denominated in developed-market currencies (such as the U.S. dollar), issued in international markets, and subject to the issuing country’s laws. On the other hand, local currency debt is denominated in EM currencies (such as the Brazilian real or the Mexican peso) and predominantly issued in local markets under the issuing country’s laws.

In addition, sovereign debt is issued by a national government to finance its activities or manage its debt and is tied to a country’s fiscal health and political stability. Corporate debt is issued by corporations to raise capital for business operations and depends on the financial strength and profitability of the issuing company. One can also further narrow both of these categories down by focusing on frontier markets debt, which is issued by governments or corporations in frontier markets; these are economies that are less developed than emerging markets but have high growth potential.

EM debt is often tracked and analyzed through various indices, which serve as benchmarks for both hard currency and local currency debt. One of the most prominent indices for hard currency EM debt is the J.P. Morgan EMBI Global Diversified. For local currency debt, the J.P. Morgan GBI-EM Global Diversified is widely used.

These indices are crucial for investors as they provide a framework for measuring performance, assessing risk, and making informed allocation decisions within the EM debt universe. And inclusion in these indices often signals a certain level of credibility and market access for the issuing countries, which can influence investment flows and borrowing costs.

Why Invest in EM Debt?

EM debt is a sizable asset class that has substantially grown over the years. It now represents close to a quarter of the global fixed-income universe, and is diversified across countries, currencies, and corporate credit issuers.

From a credit perspective, we believe EM debt appears solid, with strong fundamentals in both sovereign and corporate credit when compared to similar issuers in developed markets. Multilateral and bilateral institutions, such as the International Monetary Fund (IMF), provide strong support via accessible and affordable financing for EM countries as well. This helps contribute to lower default rates, as countries can secure stable funding even in unfavorable market conditions.

EM debt now represents close to a quarter of the global fixed-income universe, and is diversified across countries, currencies, and corporate credit issuers.

In addition, EM debt has tended to have a lower correlation to other global asset classes, which may offer investors the potential for diversification.

However, EM debt is often misunderstood, and therefore under-owned and undervalued, in our opinion. Several factors contribute to this: perceived risk vs. actual risk, underrepresentation in global fixed-income indices, inefficient pricing, and historical biases.

We believe this misunderstanding creates potential opportunities for investors who can recognize the asset class’s true strengths and potential for alpha generation.

What are Performance Drivers of EM Debt?

We believe performance of EM debt is primarily driven by two forces: global economic growth and global liquidity conditions.

Improving economic conditions can lead to fundamental improvement in EM countries, which can result in lower risk premiums. And when global interest rates are low and global liquidity is abundant, we have seen EM debt perform well at times.

In market environments with declining inflation or falling interest rates, EM debt has also performed well, as it has tended to display a higher sensitivity to rates. In volatile market environments, EM debt has typically displayed higher volatility than other fixed-income asset classes in the developed market space due to rising risk aversion and investor outflows. But this asset class has historically performed well after periods of high market volatility.

In addition, we believe the U.S. dollar plays an important role in EM debt, and securities may benefit when the U.S. dollar depreciates.

What are Common Myths About EM Debt?

Investing in EM debt is often viewed through a lens of caution and underscored by a common narrative that emphasizes perceived heightened risk. One common myth about the asset class is, “EM debt isn’t right for my investment portfolio,” as some investors often avoid EM debt because of concerns over risk, volatility, and lack of familiarity.

However, we believe this myth, among others, tends to overshadow the nuanced realities of EM debt, and dissecting them reveals an asset class that, when navigated with insight and expertise, presents compelling opportunities for discerning investors.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: UNHappy Ending: How Big Challenges Nuked My UnitedHealth Thesis
data.description:
UnitedHealth Overview

I was wrong.

On December 5, I wrote an article with the super-long title "UnitedHealth: A Dividend Growth Heavyweight Champion Looking To Boost Growth."

Initially, my thesis looked great, as UnitedHealth Group (NYSE:UNH) did quite well in recent weeks, after showing a lot of volatility throughout the winter months.

Then, the thesis got knocked out cold, as America's largest health insurance provider lost roughly a fifth of its market cap after reporting first-quarter earnings, making me look bad.

And rightfully so, as I made a huge mistake.

In this article, I'll tell you what I did wrong and everything you need to know about the risk/reward of what used to be one of the best companies for capital gains, anti-cyclical demand (sleep well at night), and consistently rising dividends.

So, let's get to it!

UnitedHealth's Q1 2025 - The Worst Quarter In A Long Time

I have to say, I am somewhat ashamed of being this wrong, as my thesis was nuked by a factor that I actually got right.

To quote Bloomberg:

The company said it was blindsided by rising medical costs as the first quarter closed, upending a forecast it had affirmed just three months ago.

UnitedHealth fell as much as 20% when US markets opened, the most since Sept. 1999. The guidance cut is rare for UnitedHealth, which typically forecasts conservatively and often raises its outlook as the year progresses. - Bloomberg (emphasis added)

Rising medical costs and sticky inflation have been a core part of my strategy for a very long time. That's why I am so bullish on medical equipment providers and companies with pricing power.

I was just under the impression that UnitedHealth had figured out how to stick to guidance despite these headwinds. While it's no excuse, many others seemed to agree with me, as 'nobody' expected this poor EPS result, as the company missed earnings for the first time in a decade. Yes, really.

In other words, this is a case where one of the most reliable performers in the entire stock market suddenly shows weakness. This creates elevated uncertainty, as stocks like UNH are a safe haven for countless investors.

"Unexpected changes" in the company's Optum Health care delivery business that affect reimbursements and elevated care activity in Medicare Advantage health plans are not what anyone wants to see.

According to the company, the jump in quarter-end Medicare expenses was concentrated in physician and outpatient services and even outpaced the higher level of care expenses it had initially prepared for 2025.

The worst part is that a big part of the weakness was tied to Optum Health, as I already briefly mentioned. This segment operates clinics, surgery centers, and home care businesses. As this has been one of the company's fastest-growing profit sources, it's easy to imagine the disappointment among investors and analysts.

I added emphasis to the quote below:

Optum Health has almost 5 million members in insurance-like arrangements, where the company is accountable for the total cost of patients’ care. It treats these patients on behalf of UnitedHealthcare and rival insurers.

The company suggested members in its Optum Health business coming from other insurance plans “may not be appropriately risk-coded,” JPMorgan analyst Lisa Gill wrote in a research note. That would reduce revenue from Medicare. - Bloomberg

This resulted in a $10 billion revenue outlook cut for Optum to a new range of $106 billion to $107 billion.

Here are two more quotes from the company, as it vented some frustration with regard to rising costs:

[...] drug manufacturers continue to increase what they charge Americans, in some cases, 10x what they charge people in Europe.

[...]

Common procedures such as heart bypass surgery, spinal fusions, and heart stents are 4x as expensive in the U.S. as they are in Germany, Australia, and the U.K. Total hip replacements are twice as much. It's simply not sustainable. - UNH Q1 '25 Earnings Call

Moreover, during the earnings call, the company noted that the revised outlook means that about 50% of the full-year earnings will now come from the first half of the year, leaving a potentially weaker performance for the second half of 2025.

This is an issue, especially when considering that UnitedHealth had previously expected a more balanced distribution of earnings.

The good news is that the company affirmed its full-year revenue forecast of $450 billion to $455 billion, which is consistent with the guidance the company released in December.

In general, it's not all bad.

There's Some Good News

The good news is that despite everything we just mentioned, the company's UnitedHealthcare and OptumRx segments are expected to do better than expected, which helps to offset some OptumHealth headwinds.

The $10 billion revenue hit at OptumHealth is mainly attributed to a shift from legacy risk-based arrangements to fee-based arrangements, as I already briefly mentioned, which are neutral to earnings but a headwind for OptumHealth's operating performance.

Essentially, it's an operating problem, as it comes with issues managing the new CMS risk model and other operations. It's not bullish, but something that needs to be said.

In general, the company needs to focus a lot more on costs, as rising costs and the related surge in people who need care (aging population) are presenting long-term challenges for the entire industry.

That's why UnitedHealth is investing in technology, including AI-powered tools to deal with claims. While that may work, it also comes with new challenges, as rejecting care is a very tough thing to do. While it remains to be seen what the judge will rule, it is likely (almost certainly) the reason why one of its executives was assassinated in New York last year.

This incident really showed how many people truly hate the healthcare industry. And I am not using the word "hate" lightly here.

Cutting costs in an environment of rising demand is one of the toughest things to do. I do not envy these decision-makers, even if I assume some of them make way more money than me.

With that said, overall demand remains strong, as UnitedHealthcare expects to add up to 800,000 new members in its Medicare Advantage programs, which include individuals and group plans. It also benefits from new state expansions in Kentucky, New York, and the Sunshine State, Florida.

As a result, UnitedHealth is sticking to its long-term target, which sees between 13% and 16% annual earnings growth.

[...] we're committed to improving our performance in the rest of 2025 and into '26, and in doing so to delivering consistent positive results for you and returning to our long-term earnings per share growth target of 13% to 16%. - UNH Q1 '25 Earnings Call

Now, let's look into what this could mean for investors.

The Risk/Reward In UNH Stock

For what it's worth, the best thing about steep sell-offs is that a lot will be priced in.

While I am writing this, UNH yields 1.9%. This dividend has a 30% payout ratio and a five-year CAGR of 14%.

It also trades at just 13.5x 2026E EPS of $33.55, which is a mile below its multi-decade average of 17.6x (FactSet data).

Moreover, it's interesting that analysts already had lowered their expectations, forecasting just 8% EPS growth this year. 2026 and 2027 are expected to see 13-14% growth, in line with the company's longer-term guidance.

While I believe 2025 and 2026 will likely both be below 10%, I still like UNH at these levels, as the risk/reward is good.

However, because the company has made it very clear that both inflation and secular growth in costs are issues, UNH is not a high-conviction idea.

Although I think UNH has the ability to deliver substantial long-term total returns, I prefer other places to put money, especially because I believe in 'higher for longer' inflation and interest rates.

In light of these challenges, I will likely write a special article next week on inflation and secular challenges facing some of the market's most beloved stocks.

Takeaway

I made a mistake with UnitedHealth.

While the company still holds long-term potential, its recent earnings miss, especially from the Optum Health segment, has raised red flags. A lot of them.

Rising costs, especially in healthcare, remain a major challenge, and although the company is adjusting its approach, I think it's fair to say a ton of uncertainty remains.

Fortunately, for new investors, the stock is currently trading below its historical valuation, which makes it an attractive entry point for everyone looking for insurance exposure.

However, it's no longer a high-conviction pick for me.

With inflationary pressures lingering and despite a bigger focus on efficiencies, I'm cautious about its near-term outlook and prefer other opportunities that align more closely with my current strategy.

data.title: State Street Q1: Earnings Beat With Positive Outlook
data.description:
I rate State Street Corporation (NYSE:STT) a Buy, for capital appreciation-focused investors who are looking for long-term buy and hold investments. The company just declared Q1 earnings, beating the estimates, showing growth in all key metrics. Growth in assets under custody shows their business model is working by retaining clients and growing their assets. The most important factors that influence my opinion of the stock are:

  • Q1 2025 earnings were strong, showing increases in revenue and income.
  • Quarterly net income of $644 million was up +39%.
  • Assets under custody grew 6% and Assets under management grew 9% for the quarter.
  • Pays a rising quarterly dividend with an annual yield of 3.82%.
  • Business model focused on institutional clients, which tends to last many years.
  • Opportunity to grow internationally.

Seeking Alpha Quant rates the stock a Sell 2.42 (as of 4/17/2025).

Global Provider of Financial Services to Institutions

Headquartered in Boston, State Street is

“one of the world's leading providers of financial services to institutional investors including investment servicing, investment management and investment research and trading. With $46.6 trillion in assets under custody and/or administration and $4.7 trillion* in assets under management as of December 31, 2024, State Street operates globally in more than 100 geographic markets and employs approximately 53,000 worldwide.”

The company offers a broad range of financial products and services through various subsidiaries, including State Street Bank and Trust company. Their core business is investment servicing and investment management, with a large presence in ETFs.

Within State Street Global Advisors, the $4.7 trillion in AUM breaks out as shown in the chart below. The largest segment is ETFs, which are $1.6 trillion, up +24%, part of an $11 trillion market.

Q1 2025 Earnings Beat

STT reported a strong Q1 2025, kicking off the year with multiple beats vs estimates. At a high level, revenues rose 5%, expenses dropped 3%; therefore net income increased substantially +39%. Also, the key metrics surrounding client accounts (AUC/AUM) grew, which is a good sign for the company’s future.

On April 17, 2025, the company declared Q1 2025 earnings, with the following quarterly financial highlights:

  • Total revenue of $3.28 billion grew +5%, fee revenue up +6%
  • Expenses of $2.4 billion were down 3%
  • Net income of $644 million was up +39%
  • Earnings per share (EPS) of $2.04 increased +49%
  • Investment Servicing AUC/A increased 6% to $46.7 billion
  • Investment Management AUM increased 9% to $4.7 billion.

Slower Growing Performance vs. Other Financial Services

A $10,000 investment in STT held over 10 years would be worth $13,780 today. This performance falls short of the financial services sector as represented by SPDR Fund (XLF), which achieved almost double the growth. Given their slower growth asset servicing business, the upside is once they have the business it is hard to lose it; however, the business doesn't have the faster growing businesses such as trading, investment banking, or charging higher fees on retail investor accounts.

Growing Valuation

STT has a long history of increasing dividends, as shown visually in the chart below. This trend is encouraging to investors because it serves as a base for the stock. In times of volatility, the dividend becomes more valuable as the stock price goes down, making the stock more attractive as the dividend yield rises.

Over the past 10 years and more, STT has a history of growing the dividend, making it a good contender for buying and holding for the long term. For example, one share of STT earned a dividend of $1.16 in 2014. That same share now collects $3.04 in 2025.

In comparison to other financial services companies, STT’s metrics are in line with the industry. In fact, their price earnings ratio is the lowest in the group, indicating STT may be trading at a discount.

Morgan Stanley (MS) is the largest of the comparables, as ranked by market capitalization.

Blue Owl Capital Inc. (OWL), a fairly new company, has the second-highest dividend yield of 4.2%, the highest dividend growth rate over the past 3 years, and at $26 billion in market cap is the most similar in size to STT.

T. Rowe Price Group, Inc. (TROW) has the highest dividend yield of 6.0%, but the worst 3 year total return, declining -28.6%.

The Goldman Sachs Group, Inc. (GS) is the second-largest by market capitalization, with the second highest 3 year total return.

The Bank of New York Mellon Corporation (BK)has the lowest dividend yield of 2.5% but the highest 3 year total return of 79.7%.

Brookfield Asset Management Ltd. (BAM) is the newest of the group, going public on December 1, 2022. BAM trades with the highest price earnings ratio of 25.8.

Outlook

Short term, companies such as STT face uncertainty driven by government policies around trade, tasks to reduce the deficit, tax policy, and deregulation. Their clients have the same issues, which will determine which services they will need. If STT can address the issues effectively with clients, this time of stress will build stronger relationships.

Given the uncertainty in capital markets, it can be expected that fee revenue may be compromised with lower market valuations; hence AUM will go down.

STT will find future growth opportunities doing business internationally.

Risks To Rating

The main risks to STT are in government policy regarding trade, taxes, and regulation as these affect asset valuation and fee revenue. Institutional clients may react to changes in policy or position themselves in such a way to exploit opportunities or avoid threats.

A secondary risk is competition. The financial services sector is highly competitive, therefore changes in strategy from a competitor could pose a significant threat to STT. Given the lower margin business of asset servicing, combined with the core competency of their scale, puts a wide moat around the company from competition.

Bottom Line

I rate STT a "Buy" for capital appreciation focused investors looking for long-term buy and hold investments. As the majority of STT’s business is asset servicing, the business model builds in a high level of continuity, making it easier for a client to continue using STT vs. changing. The company pays a quarterly dividend, currently yielding 3.82%. STT has a history of growing the dividend consistently over time, making the investment more attractive with this feature.

Q1 2025 earnings released on April 17th were very positive, showing growth in revenues and net income, driving a beat in earnings. Further growth in assets under custody to $46.7 trillion shows the company’s strategy is working, drawing in more clients who remain at the firm.

Risks to the company are driven by government policy where trade, taxes, and regulation are currently being debated. The outlook for STT is still positive, as the institutions will adjust as needed, most likely staying with the service provider selected.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The Return Of Dividends: Why DHS Is Having A Moment
data.description:
By Christopher Gannatti, CFA

At WisdomTree, our journey began in 2006 with a foundational belief: that dividends matter. The market regime following the global financial crisis1 often rewarded high-growth, non-dividend-paying companies—particularly in the U.S. Technology, Consumer Discretionary and Communication Services sectors. A core tenet of portfolio construction became easy to ignore: the importance of defense.

For much of the past two decades, dividend strategies were overshadowed by the rise of mega-cap growth. But there's a principle in investing—defense can appear as annoying underperformance…until you need it. In 2025, with rising volatility and a difficult-to-predict Trump Administration, there is a growing market rotation away from the Magnificent 7,2 and dividend-paying companies have come back in focus.

Figure 1: Standardized Performance

So far, 2025 has been a rough ride—and in a year where everything feels a bit painful return-wise, the WisdomTree U.S. High Dividend Fund (DHS) has been less painful. Down −3.5% year-to-date (YTD), it's held up meaningfully better than the Russell 1000 Value Index (−8.1%) and especially the S&P 500 (−13.6%). The Nasdaq 100 Index has been the leader for some time, emphasizing exposure to the mega-cap technology-oriented companies we mentioned earlier, and it is down 16.9% over this YTD period. When the market starts to question the price it pays for forward-looking expectations, cash in hand—in the form of dividends—suddenly looks like a more favorable deal.

Zoom out to the one-year view, and DHS has maintained its prominence. While broad value and the S&P 500 still reflect losses (−2.3% and −1.4%, respectively), DHS is up 8.6%. Again, the Nasdaq 100 Index faced the toughest result, down 3.0%. That's not a fluke. It's a signal. Market leadership is starting to rotate, and strategies that favor durability over disruption are gaining ground.

But move further out—three, five and 10 years—and the gravitational pull of megacap tech becomes unavoidable. The S&P 500, supercharged by a handful of dominant growth stocks, lapped both dividend and value benchmarks, and the Nasdaq 100 Index did even better. It's a reminder that in certain regimes, concentration can look like genius…until it doesn't.

In our opinion, the point isn't that dividends always win. It's that they show up when you need them most—and they do so without requiring you to bet the farm on what's next in AI, semiconductors or space colonization.

Figure 2: DHS's Near-Term Outperformance as Defense Became More Important

Sometimes the standard periods of multiple years can mask the experience that many of us might remember during individual years. For example:

  • The S&P 500 Index had a negative calendar-year return during two of the last 10 full calendar years, 2018 and 2022. Many may remember 2022 in particular, as the Federal Reserve was hiking its policy rate quickly to combat inflation. U.S. equities and fixed income were challenged. While people may remember the −18.11% return of the S&P 500 Index, they may not remember that the Nasdaq 100 Index was down more than 32%.
  • DHS delivered a strongly defensive result in 2022—a positive 7.88% return while the other benchmarks in figure 3 were all negative. However, 2018 was not as successful.
  • We believe that many people have not been looking at DHS because of what is clear in 2023 and 2024, when the Fund dramatically trailed both the S&P 500 and Nasdaq 100 indexes. This is that "annoying underperformance" that we mentioned earlier.

Figure 3: The Last 10 Calendar Years

What You Don't Own Matters Just as Much as What You Do

One look at figure 4 and the story becomes obvious: DHS is not trying to be everything to everyone. It's deliberately not chasing tech. While the S&P 500 leans hard into Information Technology (north of 30% exposure), DHS sits comfortably with single-digit weight. That's not an accident—it's the product of a strategy that asks a different question: "Who's paying shareholders today?" rather than "Who's growing the fastest?"

That sector tilt—away from the frothy and toward the fundamental—defines the defensive DNA of DHS. It's right there in the over-weight allocations to Consumer Staples, Utilities, Energy and Health Care. These aren't the sectors that dominate headlines, but they do tend to hold up when the headlines turn sour.

Financials and Energy together account for over 30% of DHS—two sectors that, while cyclical, also represent valuation discipline and free cash flow. These are businesses that have had to prove themselves in high-rate environments. And in a world where capital is no longer free, that starts to matter again.

The under-weight to Information Technology, and to a lesser extent to Communication Services, has felt like a liability in the growth-dominated years. But in 2025, it's looking more like a cushion.

Figure 4: DHS Emphasized a Defensive Sector Orientation

When You Don't Own the Magnificent 7

Look at DHS's top holdings. There's no Apple. No Nvidia (NVDA). No Microsoft (MSFT). No Alphabet (GOOGL), Amazon (AMZN), Meta (META) or Tesla (TSLA). In fact, the total exposure to the Magnificent 7 is zero. This is a portfolio that has deliberately not owned the market's most talked-about—and most concentrated—drivers of return over the past five years.

Names like Philip Morris (PM), Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), Exxon Mobil (XOM) and AbbVie (ABBV) - these are household names, yes, but also steady compounders with strong balance sheets and consistent dividend policies. In fact, the top 10 names in DHS account for over 42% of the portfolio, and every one of them pays you to own them.

Contrast that with the S&P 500, where more than a third of the index is concentrated in just 10 companies—seven of which are tech-driven giants. That's a bet on continued dominance by a handful of firms that have already defied gravity for a decade.

The Russell 1000 Value Index may be Value, but its top holdings do overlap with those of the S&P 500 Index and total only about 18% of the portfolio.

DHS expresses something different: intentional concentration in income-generating companies. It's not designed to beat the S&P 500 Index in momentum-driven bull markets. It's designed to act like ballast when the future starts to feel uncertain.

Figure 5: DHS Avoided the Magnificent 7

The Payoff: Real Income, Right Now

DHS offers a 3.88% dividend yield3—nearly double the Russell 1000 Value Index and more than triple the S&P 500. That's not a rounding error. That's a fundamentally different approach to equity investing.

Where most portfolios today are implicitly betting on multiple expansion, DHS delivers a return stream that doesn't require perfect timing or endless growth. It pays you to be patient. It pays you through the volatility.

Importantly, this isn't yield for yield's sake. These are not distressed businesses with unsustainable payout ratios. DHS is built from the ground up and seeks to mitigate the risk of exposure to value traps and companies paying unsustainable dividends. The highest weight is not in companies with the highest yields but rather in those companies that are distributing the most cash in aggregate to their shareholders.

In a world where cash once again has a cost and equity markets are repricing risk, income is strategy—not just an outcome.

Figure 6: DHS Tilted toward Higher Dividend-Yielding Companies

Valuation Discipline in an Expensive Market

With the S&P 500 trading at 22 times forward earnings, investors are once again leaning heavily on the idea that growth will justify the price. The Nasdaq 100 Index saw an even higher valuation. The Russell 1000 Value Index is more modest, but still sits at 17.5 times. Neither feels particularly cheap by historical standards, especially with real rates now positive and capital no longer free.

Then there's DHS—clocking in at just 14.0 times.

That number isn't just lower; it's anchored in a portfolio of dividend payers with high return-on-equity metrics and a strict valuation discipline at the time of rebalancing. It reflects a different logic: don't pay up for potential, pay less for profitability that already exists.

In an era where multiple expansion may be limited, we believe starting valuation matters more than ever.

Figure 7: DHS Dialed Down the Estimated P/E Ratio

Conclusion: When the Market Narrative Changes, So Should the Toolkit

For much of the last decade, being under-weight in tech or over-weight in dividends felt like a drag—an anchor in a market that rewarded speed over stability, potential over profit. But in 2025, the narrative is shifting, and DHS is meeting the moment.

In a market defined by extreme concentration, extended valuations and rising macroeconomic uncertainty, DHS offers something refreshingly scarce: balance. It is a portfolio built not on dreams of disruption but on durable cash flows, capital return and valuation discipline. The Fund has delivered strong relative performance in a tough environment, and its sector and stock positioning shows a clear intentionality: real businesses, paying real income, at reasonable prices.

Where others are asking, "How much longer can the Magnificent 7 lead?", DHS has already moved on.

1 The global financial crisis played out in global markets during portions of 2008 and 2009. After this period, interest rates across much of the developed world were at or near record lows for extended periods, an environment that was very favorable for growth-oriented equities.

2 Refers to Apple, Amazon.com, Alphabet, Microsoft, Meta Platforms, Nvidia and Tesla.

3 As of 4/15/25, DHS's SEC 30-day Yield = 3.94%.

Christopher Gannatti, CFA, Global Head of Research

Christopher Gannatti began at WisdomTree as a Research Analyst in December 2010, working directly with Jeremy Schwartz, CFA®, Director of Research. In January of 2014, he was promoted to Associate Director of Research where he was responsible to lead different groups of analysts and strategists within the broader Research team at WisdomTree. In February of 2018, Christopher was promoted to Head of Research, Europe, where he was based out of WisdomTree's London office and was responsible for the full WisdomTree research effort within the European market, as well as supporting the UCITs platform globally. In November 2021, Christopher was promoted to Global Head of Research, now responsible for numerous communications on investment strategy globally, particularly in the thematic equity space. Christopher came to WisdomTree from Lord Abbett, where he worked for four and a half years as a Regional Consultant. He received his MBA in Quantitative Finance, Accounting, and Economics from NYU's Stern School of Business in 2010, and he received his bachelor's degree from Colgate University in Economics in 2006. Christopher is a holder of the Chartered Financial Analyst Designation.

data.title: FLEE: Not A Bad Bet, If You Can Overlook Liquidity Challenges
data.description:
Introduction

The Franklin FTSE Europe ETF (NYSEARCA:FLEE) which focuses on over 500 stocks (mainly giant and large-caps which account for 85% of the total portfolio) from developed economies across Europe has kicked off 2025 on a pretty good note. After three-and-a-half months of trading, where global markets are down by over mid-single-digits, FLEE has notched positive returns within the double-digit threshold.

If you’re intrigued by FLEE’s relatively comfortable return profile, and are open to pursuing this product, here are some important sub-plots worth noting.

How Does FLEE Compare To VGK, The Most Popular Europe ETF Around?

To better understand FLEE’s characteristics, we’ve stacked it up against the Vanguard FTSE Europe ETF (VGK), which is largely perceived to be the go-to ETF vehicle for those looking to tap developed market European equities. This is exemplified by a stunning AUM figure of almost $28B, which is a whopping 324x more than FLEE’s AUM.

While both products focus on developed market stocks from Europe, they both track different indices; FLEE tracks the FTSE Developed Europe RIC Capped Index, while VGK tracks the FTSE Developed Europe All Cap Index. The main difference between these two indices is the capping methodology employed. Basically, FLEE’s tracking index seeks to employ quarterly caps on its holdings so that there are no concentration effects. However, note that VGK’s tracking index is wider in scope, covering 2.4x the number of stocks that FLEE covers (a little over 500), so even here you don’t really find too many concentration effects. The top 10 of both products is fairly well controlled at less than 20%, while even from a geographic and sector angle, there are no major tilts. Both products are primarily exposed to the UK (21% weight for FLEE and 24% for VGK), and the financial sector (22% exposure for both products). From a market-cap angle, the biggest takeaway is that VGK gives you some exposure to small-caps (mid-single-digit exposure), while FLEE doesn’t dabble with that cohort at all.

One of the chief reasons to stay away from FLEE is that it suffers from significant liquidity issues, and this could be a problem for those who gravitate toward making short-term tactical bets. With daily dollar volumes averaging less than $1m, spreads between the bid and the ask of FLEE are inordinately wide at 0.52%. The same can’t be said for VGK, which witnesses dollar volumes of over $21m per day (on average), and offers exceptional spreads of 0.01%.

From a cost angle too, FLEE comes up short, with an expense ratio that is twice as high as what VGK is priced at (0.06%). Both products are exceptionally stable with the annual churn of low single-digits, although once again VGK has a slight edge with a turnover ratio of just 3%.

If you’re someone who attaches a lot of importance to the income facet of an ETF, you’re probably to be inclined to go with FLEE, given its superior yields, both as of today, as well as its 4-year average. However, do take note of VGK’s recent generosity where its dividends over the last three years have been growing at over 3x the pace of VGK’s dividends.

When it comes to the relative performance of these two products, once again it is the larger product which comes out on top (since FLEE got listed), with the current price differential amounting to almost 350bps in favor of VGK.

With regard to the volatility profile of these two products, there isn’t an awful lot separating them, although currently, FLEE appears to come across as slightly more comforting.

If we may drill down further, we could perhaps ascertain the quality of returns; by this we mean, considering the risk taken by both products to deliver the returns, both over a 3-year time frame as well as a 5-year time frame. The proxy for this will be the Sharpe ratio, and here the takeaway is that for the total risk that FLEE employs, it delivers a superior threshold of excess returns (over the risk-free rate) than VGK.

What Do The Macros Look Like?

If it is growth that you’re after, Europe probably isn’t the most appropriate terrain to dive into. While the globe will likely see its GDP grow by over 3% this year, and the US could see growth of 2.7% (although there are plenty of downside risks to this number, given the ongoing tariff developments), Europe is only poised to grow at a level closer to 1%. In fact, the ECB which has come out with more recent forecasts, believes that real GDP growth here could only be closer to 0.9%, which is pretty much in line with the growth rate of last year.

While the growth outlook may not be very eye-popping, European assets are bound to see improved interest from the investment community given the underlying desire of the authorities there to ramp up stimulus. Germany, the largest economy in Europe is poised to infuse a multi-year EUR500B spending package, which would account for a double-digit share of the country’s total GDP. Meanwhile, the EU too plans to beef up its defense capabilities by potentially spending around EUR800B. Financing conditions are also getting a lot cheaper in Europe with the policy rate poised to drop for the seventh time since June 2024 to levels of 2.25%. This is unlikely to be the last with another one or two 25bps cuts expected through 2025.

This should do a world of good for European households, given that the debt-to-income ratio is at rather elevated levels of 96%.

Having said all that, we are quite troubled to note that the latest ZEW Economic Sentiment index for April dropped to negative territory for the first time since September 2023, and to its lowest level since December 2022. Much of this is being driven by the fears of a tariff war, but with a not-too-strenuous reciprocal tariff rate of 20% for the EU, and a 90-day reprieve, the region is better prepared than most regions to weather the storm.

What Do The Valuations Look Like?

Despite the rise this year, FLEE’s valuations still look rather compelling. To highlight, FLEE’s portfolio of stocks is poised to deliver long-term earnings growth that is not too dissimilar to what the globe is poised to witness (a differential of less than 100 bps). Yet for a similar degree of earnings growth, FLEE’s holdings are priced at a weighted average forward P/E discount of 17% relative to the corresponding P/E of VT (the ETF proxy for the globe).

Closing Thoughts: What Do The Charts Suggest?

FLEE’s cheap valuations could potentially drive interest to this fund, but do consider that its relative strength [RS] ratio versus the globe is almost close to mean-reverting.

As opposed to its long-term average of less than 0.29x, the current ratio is only around 4% lower. That was certainly not the case earlier this year when it was at record lows and looked like a more dazzling mean-reversion prospect.

Separately, if one looks at FLEE’s own weekly movements over the last 30 months, one would note that the ETF has been moving higher, but within the boundaries of a certain ascending channel. A couple of weeks back, FLEE was in danger of dropping below its lower channel boundary, but managed to display strong fire-fighting qualities and bounced back well enough. At the current price levels, we view the risk-reward as fair, as the distance with the channel boundaries, both to the upside and downside is relatively fair at around 8-9%.

data.title: RSPS: Consumer Staples Dashboard For April
data.description:
This monthly article series reports industry metrics in the consumer staples sector, aiming at a top-down analysis of value, quality and momentum. It may also help analyze sector ETFs such as The Consumer Staples Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLP) and Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight Consumer Staples ETF (NYSEARCA:RSPS), whose holdings are used to calculate these metrics.

Shortcut

The next two paragraphs in italic describe the dashboard methodology. They are necessary for new readers to understand the metrics. If you are used to this series or if you are short of time, you can skip them and go to the charts.

Base Metrics

I calculate the median value of five fundamental ratios for each industry: Earnings Yield ("EY"), Sales Yield ("SY"), Free Cash Flow Yield ("FY"), Return on Equity ("ROE"), Gross Margin ("GM"). The reference universe includes large companies in the U.S. stock market. The five base metrics are calculated on trailing 12 months. For all of them, higher is better. EY, SY and FY are medians of the inverse of Price/Earnings, Price/Sales and Price/Free Cash Flow. They are better for statistical studies than price-to-something ratios, which are unusable or non-available when the "something" is close to zero or negative (for example, companies with negative earnings). I also look at two momentum metrics for each group: the median monthly return (RetM) and the median annual return (RetY).

I prefer medians to averages because a median splits a set in a good half and a bad half. A capital-weighted average is skewed by extreme values and the largest companies. My metrics are designed for stock-picking rather than index investing.

Value and Quality Scores

I calculate historical baselines for all metrics. They are noted respectively EYh, SYh, FYh, ROEh, GMh, and they are calculated as the averages on a look-back period of 11 years. For example, the value of EYh for food in the table below is the 11-year average of the median Earnings Yield in food companies.

The Value Score ("VS") is defined as the average difference in % between the three valuation ratios (EY, SY, FY) and their baselines (EYh, SYh, FYh). The same way, the Quality Score ("QS") is the average difference between the two quality ratios (ROE, GM) and their baselines (ROEh, GMh).

The scores are in percentage points. VS may be interpreted as the percentage of undervaluation or overvaluation relative to the baseline (positive is good, negative is bad). This interpretation must be taken with caution: the baseline is an arbitrary reference, not a supposed fair value. The formula assumes that the three valuation metrics are of equal importance.
